transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, how are you doing? TV and Radio Thomas Vance here. Welcome, once again, to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, archive sessions by a man whom some like and some do not.
He's a singer-songwriter who, since he started recording in 1966, has played with some of Britain's finest musicians.
His name is Roy Harper.
Also, we have the first ever recorded session for the BBC by Girl.
We've got classic cuts by Kevin Ayres, Black Sabbath, Rainbow and Gary Moore.
And new releases from Toto, Uriah Heep and a French band by the name of Heldon.
Not forgetting, of course, our Friday Night Connection.
And tonight, I promise you, I will play all the tracks prescribed.
This evening, we start with The Motors.
[Music]
[Music]
[Music]
And that was a track from the new album by Toto, which is called Hydra.
It's just come out on the CBS label and the track was called White Sister.
And that's a really fine American band that includes some of the sons of some of America's greatest ever session band.
And prior to that, a track by The Motors from their 1977 debut album called The Motors.
The track was Dancing the Light Away.
Whatever happened to The Motors, they really fell by the wayside.
So indeed did Bram Tchaikovsky, The Offshoot.
Nonetheless, we played that track by The Motors for Dave Telford and Fungus Bryn Bonham
and Crawfee Babe Duncan and John the Bank Harris and Dave and Sarah
and Tog and Nat and Kev
and all the good music lovers at Itchen College in Southampton.
10-14, The Floyd.
[Music]
And that was a track that came out in 1974
on an album called The Confessions of Dr. Dream.
It was the Dr. Dream theme by Kevin Ayres.
Most especially pulled out of the BBC Record Library,
that home of millions of pieces of good music for Nick Bishop,
at 15 Grove Avenue in Harpenden in Hertfordshire.
It was also for a lady by the name of Wendy in Worthing,
who's in the bar as always at this time of night on a friday and before that something from the
wall by of course the pink floyd for jebo for snack and also for dave three lonely and desperate
lads who are well to the front of the mind of a girl called mandy also related to stephen kellett
of number two kirk b road i think it is in culcheth waddington in cheshire and also for
ian wallace of redcar 26 minutes after 10 o'clock it's the friday rock show and now i'd like to tell
you about something that we're going to be doing on the 8th of february we're going to be starting
a sort of every once in a while series which is going to be called flashback and on the february
the 8th we're going to be doing flashback 67 the whole of the two-hour period of the friday rock
show will be interwoven around the music of 1967 now if you can think of a track that relates to
that particular year that's a favorite of yours would you let me know write it on a card and send
to flashback 67 the friday rock show bbc radio 1 london w1a4 ww okay your favorite track from
1967 to the friday rock show bbc radio 1 london w1a4 ww and if you can't think of one then ask
your dad because i'm sure he can and now here's the first of four by girl from 1976 from the album
[Music]
[Music]
technical ecstasy and dirty women not too often do we get around to playing black sabbath but when
we do my headphones ring with appreciation as indeed i would think to the years of stewart
jefferson of 11 i know that's 14 just up the road st leonard street lanark in scotland who wanted
to hear that track so did tony of scunthorpe and he wants me to dedicate that to his kid
who's in hospital somewhere during the month of january i hope it all gets better for you
Also we've played that for John Caldicott of Brown's Green,
Umberslade, Hockley Heath in the West Midlands,
and for Stuart Wallace of No. 22 Elton Street, Redcar in Cleveland.
And before that you heard the first of four from one of our sessions tonight
by Girl, who are a band who are currently on tour with UFO around the country,
and I hear that with every gig they're getting better.
The track you heard was my number.
Back now to the 14th of the 2nd of 1973
for the first of three archive sets from Roy Harper.
[Music]
A couple of tracks from a session that he did on the 14th of the 2nd of that particular year,
South Africa and Little Lady.
And the song that also is their single, which is called Do You Love Me.
And before that, you heard Kill the King from Rainbow from their album on stage.
And that was especially from Margaretha Pacey, who had her birthday on Wednesday.
Happy birthday for last Wednesday.
And she wants me to say hello to everybody at the Upper Six at Samuel Lloyd Comprehensive School,
especially those who went to Dorchester last September,
and everybody who's going to see Rainbow at Leicester in March.
That should be a good occasion.
Margareta, I hope you have a lovely birthday.
Really.
Also, Rainbow was for the Bristol University sailing team
and all the supporters of The Bust,
of which, of course, I'm one,
especially Mal and Julian and Clintz and David and Ralph and Kate and Merv and Sue,
and tell them to get back in training and to stop drinking so much.
It was also for Jack Russell and Tom Atherton
and Toby Maudsley of Morrison House.
Get this, Giggleswick School, near Settle in North Yorkshire.
And who cares about the prefects?
And finally for Neil the Hustler, the officer commanding the Marlborough Avenue platoon
of the 10th Battalion of the Alabama Pheasant Pluckers.
Got that one out all right.
It's time now for our Friday Night Connection.
Last week the tracks we played, well, most of the tracks we played were
On the Air by Peter Gabriel, Radio by Steve Hillich,
And then Space Station No. 5 by Montrose.
But I didn't actually play that.
I played the wrong track.
But I told you what the right track was.
I actually played Bad Motor Scooter.
It should have been Space Station No. 5 by Montrose.
On the air, radio, Space Station No. 5.
The answer, radio, station, communications, etc.
And the winner, the person that gets the £10 record token, is Hugh Jones.
Well done, sir.
Of 256 Weyhill Road, Andover in Hampshire.
Hampshire.
Thank you, pardon me.
Get yourself a £10 record token, congratulations
And also you've put together a Friday Night Connection of your own
Three bits of music that have a connection
And it starts off with my favourite track of last year
The Friday Night Connection put together by Hugh Jones
[Music]
Of 256 Weyhill Road, Andover in Hampshire
Mr Jones, it was a pleasure
Somewhere within those three bits of music
There is a definite connection, there is a theme
There is an umbilical cord, call it what you like
If you know what it is, jot down your answer on a postcard
in a letter, send it on telegram, we don't mind
just so long as you send it to us
that is, the Friday Night Connection
the Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1
London W1A4WW
but don't forget
put together your own Friday Night Connection as well
three bits of music with a theme
and you might win yourself a £10 record token
and more power to your elbow
it's about 11.13 and a bit
can I remind you about a programme
that we're going to be doing on February the 8th
which is the first of a sort of, well, a series of programs.
The first one is called Flashback 1967.
The whole two hours is going to be built around the music of 1967.
We'd like to know your favorite track from that particular era.
So if you jot that down on a postcard as well, we'd be grateful.
And if you'd address that postcard to Flashback 67,
that would make it on an administrative level easier.
A bit of information here.
In that program, we will be featuring
Session Stuff by Hendrix,
Cream,
Who,
And the Pink Floyd, that's on February the 8th.
And now back to Girl.
It's a new single that's just come out.
It's on the bronze label, and it's nice to see some new product,
well, newish product, if you like, by Uriah Heep.
A single called Carry On.
And before that, you heard something from our first ever session by Girl.
The track was lovely, Lorraine.
This is Frank Zappa, and you owe it to yourself
to listen to Star Special on Sunday at 7 o'clock on Radio 1
because it's going to be simply the best radio program you've ever heard in your life.
I'd say absolutely both.
That's Frank Zappa on Star Special this coming Sunday at 7 o'clock,
275-285 and stereo, VHF.
Back in 74, Roy Harper did this.
[Music]
[Music]
and I played it for Stephen Knowles, who lives at 36,
I think it's Lenehan Park in St. Phil Road, Belfast, in Northern Ireland.
I'd also like to mention a few people who are on this card.
That's Jerry Peel and Matthew Shirley of Study 12, Littleton, Stowe School, Buckingham.
And they want to say hello to all the fellow sufferers
and a special happy fourth birthday to their good mate by the name of Clank.
And also, I must mention this gentleman
His name is Richard Hender, he's age 15
This is going to be his last Friday Night Rock show for some time
Because he's off to Hong Kong for the next two years
Richard, I hate to tell you, but every weekend in Hong Kong
I do the Top 20 show, so you can't get away
19 to Midnight, and our final one by Girl
That's the old King's number, called You Really Got Me
Performed by Girl
Who are Phil Collin on lead guitar
Philip Lewis on lead vocals
Jerry Laffey on guitar
Simon Laffey on bass
and Dave Gaynor on drums.
Tomorrow night you can see them in Birmingham.
Then they do Coventry, Sheffield, Manchester,
Hanley, Bristol, Southampton,
a couple of dates in London, the 5th and 6th of February.
And they, of course, are supporting at the moment UFO.
They have an album which is on the market
on the Jet record label which is called Sheer Greed.
But may I say, and this may sound like a grovel,
but it ain't, even though he is behind me.
I think the session as produced for this show tonight
by Tony Wilson is even better than the album.
Back to 75 and Roy Harper.
And Trigger, as recorded on the 10th of June, 1975.
Trigger were Chris Spedding on the lead guitar,
Dave Cochran on the bass, and Bill Bruford on the drums.
And we dug out that archive session,
especially for Simon Middleton of St. Albans in Hertfordshire,
for Dave Howe of Clacton-on-Sea in Essex
and for Keith Chivers of Canterbury in Kent
who wants to be remembered to some mates of his,
Nick and Tony Ruby
and also a gentleman he hasn't seen for a long, long time
by the name of John Adie.
And we also played it for Jay Soanes,
P. Soaney, Jay Waters, I think it is,
and Dee Tooth.
And finally for Rajan Anand
who lives in Port Rush in County Antrim
in Northern Ireland.
It's coming up to five minutes to midnight
and to close, here's just part
of a very, very long piece of music
by a French band called
Heldon.
If you like that sort of heavy music, you'll find that on an album
which is on the Egg Label.
And it's by Heldon,
and it's called Stand By.
And that's it. Heavy version
of the program tonight. Hope you enjoyed it.
We did in the studio here.
Now, Barclay James Harvest next week
has an archive session, and a repeat of that session
we had by Terranova.
Our engineers tonight were Eddie and Paul
and John Pelaw on Monday's got The Piranhas and P.I.L.
He's also got the new select album
and the new album by The Flying Lizards.
That's on Monday with Mr. Pelaw.
Rock on Saturday, tomorrow between 4 and 7.30,
interviews with John and Vangelis,
Lena Lovitch and Nazareth,
plus the new Rainbow single
and some new bands from Australia.
My name is Tommy Vance and I'm here to tell you,
the whole of the country, that is,
that Tony Wilson, our producer tonight,
has nothing to say.
Go on, Tony, say nothing.
Nothing.
Thank you.
Wherever you are in this country of ours, wherever you are in the world, take care.
I'll see you at 4 o'clock tomorrow here on Radio 1.
From me, Tommy Vance in London, good night and God bless.
